content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:6456829e

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Contains durable user context/preferences and experiences about tomato gardening and cooking, plus a richer transcript useful as a note/source digest.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements require resolving the stable user entity; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=my garden; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=LOCATION; name=User's garden; reason=The transcript repeatedly refers to the user's garden as the source of tomatoes and tomato plants, a reusable personal location.
- mention=my tomato plants / 5 tomato plants; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's tomato plants; reason=First-person possessive wording identifies the user's tomato plants as a reusable personal item/entity for later claims.
- mention=fresh tomatoes / tomatoes / homegrown tomatoes / harvest; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's homegrown tomatoes; reason=The user describes tomatoes from their garden and harvest as personal produce, distinct from generic tomatoes.
- mention=Bruschetta; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=CONCEPT; name=Bruschetta; reason=Bruschetta is a recipe/dish the user selected to try first; no existing candidate is available.
- mention=Tomato and Mozzarella Salad; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=CONCEPT; name=Tomato and Mozzarella Salad; reason=The dish is a recipe the user selected to try first; no existing candidate is available.
- mention=homemade pasta sauce / Fresh Tomato Pasta Sauce Recipe; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=CONCEPT; name=Fresh tomato pasta sauce; reason=The transcript discusses a reusable cooking concept/recipe using the user's tomatoes; no existing candidate is available.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|tomato plants|tomatoes|garden|homemade pasta sauce|Bruschetta|Tomato and Mozzarella Salad
        text_queries=fresh tomatoes garden harvest tomato plants overwatering yellow wilt leaves watering schedule homegrown tomatoes|user planted 5 tomato plants harvesting tomatoes almost daily salads sandwiches pasta sauce|Bruschetta Tomato and Mozzarella Salad fresh tomatoes preference
        predicate_hints=prefers|has_experience_with|owns|current_metric_value|metric_observation

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/20 (Sat) 05:52
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some recipe ideas that feature fresh tomatoes as the main ingredient.
assistant: Here are some delicious recipe ideas that showcase fresh tomatoes as the main ingredient:

**Appetizers and Snacks**

1. **Bruschetta**: Fresh tomatoes, basil, garlic, mozzarella, and balsamic glaze on toasted bread.
2. **Tomato and Mozzarella Salad**: Sliced tomatoes, mozzarella, and basil, dressed with olive oil and balsamic vinegar.
3. **Tomato and Avocado Salsa**: Dice fresh tomatoes, avocados, onions, jalapeños, cilantro, and lime juice for a fresh and flavorful dip.

**Soups and Stews**

1. **Fresh Tomato Soup**: Blend fresh tomatoes, onions, garlic, and vegetable broth for a creamy and comforting soup.
2. **Tomato and Basil Gazpacho**: A cold soup made with fresh tomatoes, bell peppers, cucumbers, and basil, perfect for hot summer days.

**Pasta and Risotto**

1. **Spaghetti with Fresh Tomato Sauce**: Quickly sauté garlic, onions, and fresh tomatoes with olive oil, then toss with spaghetti and basil.
2. **Tomato and Burrata Pasta**: Toss cooked pasta with fresh tomatoes, creamy burrata cheese, and basil.
3. **Tomato and Saffron Risotto**: Add fresh tomatoes to an aromatic risotto with saffron, garlic, and parmesan cheese.

**Main Courses**

1. **Grilled Chicken and Tomato Salad**: Top grilled chicken with a salad of sliced fresh tomatoes, mozzarella, and basil, dressed with balsamic glaze.
2. **Tomato and Eggplant Parmesan**: Layer sliced fresh tomatoes, eggplant, and mozzarella in a baked casserole with marinara sauce.
3. **Stuffed Tomatoes**: Core and fill fresh tomatoes with a mixture of cooked rice, ground meat, and spices, then bake until tender.

**Sandwiches and Wraps**

1. **BLT with Fresh Tomato**: Add a juicy fresh tomato slice to the classic bacon, lettuce, and mayonnaise sandwich.
2. **Tomato and Avocado Grilled Cheese**: A gourmet twist on the classic grilled cheese, with sliced fresh tomatoes and avocado.
3. **Tomato and Feta Wrap**: Fill a wrap with sliced fresh tomatoes, crumbled feta cheese, and mixed greens.

**Breakfast and Brunch**

1. **Tomato and Spinach Omelette**: Whip up an omelette with fresh tomatoes, spinach, and feta cheese.
2. **Tomato and Basil Quiche**: Add fresh tomatoes and basil to a quiche filling with eggs, cream, and cheese.

Enjoy your tomato bounty!
user: That's quite a list! I think I'll try the Bruschetta and Tomato and Mozzarella Salad recipes first. Do you have any tips on how to store fresh tomatoes to keep them fresh for a longer period?
assistant: Bruschetta and Tomato and Mozzarella Salad are both delicious and easy to make.

Now, about storing fresh tomatoes: Here are some tips to help you keep your tomatoes fresh for a longer period:

1. **Store at room temperature**: Tomatoes should not be refrigerated, as the cold temperature can cause a loss of flavor and texture. Instead, store them at room temperature, away from direct sunlight.
2. **Keep them stem-side down**: Store tomatoes with the stem end facing down to prevent moisture from entering the fruit and causing rot.
3. **Don't wash before storing**: Excess moisture can cause tomatoes to spoil faster. Instead, gently rinse them just before using.
4. **Store in a paper bag or breathable container**: Paper bags or containers with holes allow for airflow and can help maintain humidity, keeping tomatoes fresh. Avoid storing tomatoes in plastic bags or airtight containers, as they can trap moisture and cause spoilage.
5. **Use within a few days**: Fresh tomatoes typically last 3-5 days at room temperature. Check on them daily, and use any that are ripe or nearly ripe.
6. **Ripen tomatoes at room temperature**: If you have unripe tomatoes, store them at room temperature, and they will ripen within a few days.
7. **Freeze or can excess tomatoes**: If you have an abundance of tomatoes, consider freezing or canning them to enjoy throughout the year. There are many resources available online for freezing and canning tomatoes.

Some additional tips:

* Handle tomatoes gently to avoid bruising, which can cause them to spoil faster.
* Avoid storing tomatoes near strong-smelling foods, as they can absorb odors easily.
* If you notice any mold or soft spots on a tomato, remove it from the others to prevent spoilage.

By following these tips, you should be able to enjoy your fresh tomatoes for a longer period. Happy cooking!
user: I've been using my fresh tomatoes in salads and sandwiches, but I was thinking of trying to make some homemade pasta sauce. Do you have any tips on how to make a good pasta sauce from scratch using fresh tomatoes?
assistant: Making pasta sauce from scratch using fresh tomatoes is a great way to enjoy the flavors of your garden harvest! Here are some tips to help you create a delicious and authentic homemade pasta sauce:

**Choose the right tomatoes**: For pasta sauce, you'll want to use tomatoes that are high in acidity and have a meaty texture. Plum tomatoes or San Marzano tomatoes are ideal, as they have less water content and more flesh, making them perfect for reducing and creating a rich, thick sauce.

**Roast the tomatoes**: Roasting the tomatoes brings out their natural sweetness and adds depth to the sauce. Cut the tomatoes in half, toss with olive oil, salt, and your choice of aromatics (e.g., garlic, onions, carrots, celery), and roast at 300°F (150°C) for 20-30 minutes.

**Use a flavorful base**: Saute onions, garlic, and sometimes carrots and celery in olive oil until they're soft and fragrant. This mixture, known as "soffritto," is the foundation of many Italian sauces.

**Add the roasted tomatoes**: Once the soffritto is ready, add the roasted tomatoes, salt, and a pinch of sugar to balance the acidity. You can also add a can of crushed or diced tomatoes for added depth.

**Acidity and sweetness**: A good pasta sauce needs a balance of acidity and sweetness. Tomatoes provide natural acidity, while a pinch of sugar helps balance the flavor. You can also add a splash of red wine or lemon juice for added depth.

**Simmer and reduce**: Let the sauce simmer for at least 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, to allow the flavors to meld together and the sauce to thicken. You can help the process by occasionally mashing the tomatoes with a potato masher or the back of a spoon.

**Season and finish**: Taste and adjust the seasoning as needed. You can add herbs like basil, oregano, or parsley for added freshness. Finally, finish the sauce with a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil for a rich, velvety texture.

**Tips and Variations**:

* Use a mixture of tomato varieties for a more complex flavor profile.
* Add meat or vegetables like ground beef, pork, or mushrooms for added heartiness.
* Experiment with spices like red pepper flakes, cinnamon, or nutmeg to create unique flavor profiles.
* Make the sauce ahead and store it in the fridge for up to 3 days or freeze for up to 6 months.

Here's a simple recipe to get you started:

**Fresh Tomato Pasta Sauce Recipe**

Ingredients:

* 2 lbs fresh tomatoes, cored and halved
* 2 tablespoons olive oil
* 1 onion, finely chopped
* 3 cloves garlic, minced
* 1 teaspoon salt
* 1/2 teaspoon sugar
* 1/4 cup red wine (optional)
* Fresh basil leaves, chopped (optional)

Instructions:

1. Roast the tomatoes with olive oil, salt, and aromatics (if using) at 300°F (150°C) for 20-30 minutes.
2. Saute the onion and garlic in olive oil until soft and fragrant.
3. Add the roasted tomatoes, salt, sugar, and red wine (if using) to the pot.
4. Simmer for at least 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has thickened and the flavors have melded together.
5. Taste and adjust the seasoning as needed.
6. Finish with a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil and chopped fresh basil (if using).

Enjoy your homemade pasta sauce!
user: I've heard that using fresh tomatoes can make a huge difference in the flavor of the pasta sauce. I'm excited to try out this recipe. I've been getting a lot of tomatoes from my garden lately, so this is perfect timing. Speaking of which, do you have any tips on how to keep my tomato plants healthy and productive throughout the season?
assistant: Using fresh tomatoes makes a huge difference in the flavor and texture of the pasta sauce. I'm thrilled to hear that you're excited to try out the recipe!

Now, about keeping your tomato plants healthy and productive throughout the season: Here are some tips to help you get the most out of your tomato plants:

**1. Soil and Sunlight**: Tomatoes need well-draining, fertile soil with a pH between 6.0 and 6.8. Ensure your plants receive at least 6 hours of direct sunlight per day.

**2. Water Consistently**: Tomatoes need consistent moisture, especially when fruiting. Water them deeply once or twice a week, depending on weather conditions. Avoid overwatering, which can lead to disease and root rot.

**3. Fertilize Regularly**: Feed your tomato plants with a balanced fertilizer (e.g., 10-10-10 NPK) once a month. You can also side-dress with calcium nitrate to prevent blossom-end rot.

**4. Prune and Train**: Prune your tomato plants to promote air circulation, prevent disease, and encourage fruiting. Remove lower leaves, weak growth, and any suckers (shoots that grow between the main stem and a branch). Train indeterminate varieties using tomato cages, trellises, or stakes.

**5. Mulch and Compost**: Mulch around the base of your plants to retain moisture, suppress weeds, and regulate soil temperature. Add compost to the soil to improve its structure and fertility.

**6. Monitor for Pests and Diseases**: Keep an eye out for common tomato pests like hornworms, aphids, and whiteflies. Regularly inspect your plants for signs of disease like yellowing leaves, black spots, or powdery mildew.

**7. Provide Support**: Tomatoes need support as they grow. Use tomato cages, trellises, or stakes to keep them upright and encourage even fruiting.

**8. Rotate Crops**: Rotate your tomato crop annually to avoid depleting the soil of nutrients and to reduce the risk of disease buildup.

**9. Keep the Area Weed-Free**: Weeds compete with your tomato plants for water, nutrients, and light. Regularly remove weeds to ensure your plants receive the resources they need.

**10. Learn to Identify Common Issues**: Educate yourself on common tomato problems, such as blossom-end rot, cracking, or sun scald, and take action promptly to address them.

By following these tips, you should be able to keep your tomato plants healthy and productive throughout the season. Happy gardening!

Do you have any specific questions about tomato care or common issues you've encountered in your garden?
user: I've been doing my best to keep my tomato plants healthy, but I did notice that some of the leaves started to turn yellow and wilt recently. I did some research and realized I might be overwatering them. So, I cut back on the watering schedule, and it seems to be working - the plants are looking healthier again.
assistant: You're a quick learner! It's great that you noticed the issue, did some research, and took corrective action. Overwatering is a common mistake, especially when we're eager to give our plants plenty of love and attention. But, as you've learned, too much water can be detrimental to their health.

Yellowing leaves and wilting are often signs of overwatering, as the roots start to rot and the plant becomes waterlogged. By reducing the watering frequency, you've given your tomato plants a chance to recover and regain their health.

Now that you've adjusted your watering schedule, keep an eye on your plants' progress and make further adjustments as needed. Remember, it's always better to err on the side of caution and underwater slightly than to overwater and risk root rot.

Some additional tips to prevent overwatering in the future:

1. **Check the soil moisture**: Before watering, stick your finger into the soil up to the first knuckle. If the soil feels dry, it's time to water. If it's already moist, wait another day or two before watering again.
2. **Water deeply but infrequently**: Instead of watering your tomato plants every day, water them deeply once or twice a week. This encourages deep root growth and makes them more resilient to drought.
3. **Monitor weather conditions**: Adjust your watering schedule according to weather conditions. If it's been raining or extremely humid, you may not need to water as frequently.

By being mindful of your watering habits and monitoring your plants' responses, you'll become more attuned to their needs and better equipped to provide the right amount of moisture.

How's your harvest been so far? Are you enjoying the fruits of your labor?
user: I've been enjoying the harvest immensely! I planted 5 tomato plants initially, and they've been producing like crazy. I've been harvesting tomatoes almost daily, and they're so delicious and flavorful. I've been using them in salads, sandwiches, and cooking up a storm in the kitchen. It's amazing how much more flavorful homegrown tomatoes are compared to store-bought ones.
assistant: That's fantastic! There's nothing quite like biting into a juicy, flavorful tomato that you've grown yourself. The taste and texture are unmatched, and it's a great feeling knowing that you've put in the effort to nurture those plants from seed to plate.

I'm thrilled to hear that your tomato plants are producing abundantly and that you're enjoying the fruits of your labor. Homegrown tomatoes truly are a game-changer when it comes to flavor and texture. The store-bought varieties just can't compare to the sweetness and tanginess of a fresh, ripe tomato picked right from your own garden.

It's great that you're using your tomatoes in a variety of dishes, from salads and sandwiches to cooked meals. Tomatoes are so versatile and can elevate any dish with their rich flavor and moisture. Have you tried any new recipes or cooking techniques with your homegrown tomatoes?

Also, I'm curious - what's your favorite way to enjoy your tomatoes? Do you have a go-to recipe or preparation method that brings out their flavor and texture?

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.
<env>
Project path: gromozeka-e2e-<id>
Is directory a git repo: No
Platform: Mac OS X
OS Version: 26.5.1
Today's date: <date>
</env>

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:6456829e

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Contains durable user context/preferences and experiences about tomato gardening and cooking, plus a richer transcript useful as a note/source digest.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/05/20 (Sat) 05:52
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some recipe ideas that feature fresh tomatoes as the main ingredient.
assistant: Here are some delicious recipe ideas that showcase fresh tomatoes as the main ingredient:

**Appetizers and Snacks**

1. **Bruschetta**: Fresh tomatoes, basil, garlic, mozzarella, and balsamic glaze on toasted bread.
2. **Tomato and Mozzarella Salad**: Sliced tomatoes, mozzarella, and basil, dressed with olive oil and balsamic vinegar.
3. **Tomato and Avocado Salsa**: Dice fresh tomatoes, avocados, onions, jalapeños, cilantro, and lime juice for a fresh and flavorful dip.

**Soups and Stews**

1. **Fresh Tomato Soup**: Blend fresh tomatoes, onions, garlic, and vegetable broth for a creamy and comforting soup.
2. **Tomato and Basil Gazpacho**: A cold soup made with fresh tomatoes, bell peppers, cucumbers, and basil, perfect for hot summer days.

**Pasta and Risotto**

1. **Spaghetti with Fresh Tomato Sauce**: Quickly sauté garlic, onions, and fresh tomatoes with olive oil, then toss with spaghetti and basil.
2. **Tomato and Burrata Pasta**: Toss cooked pasta with fresh tomatoes, creamy burrata cheese, and basil.
3. **Tomato and Saffron Risotto**: Add fresh tomatoes to an aromatic risotto with saffron, garlic, and parmesan cheese.

**Main Courses**

1. **Grilled Chicken and Tomato Salad**: Top grilled chicken with a salad of sliced fresh tomatoes, mozzarella, and basil, dressed with balsamic glaze.
2. **Tomato and Eggplant Parmesan**: Layer sliced fresh tomatoes, eggplant, and mozzarella in a baked casserole with marinara sauce.
3. **Stuffed Tomatoes**: Core and fill fresh tomatoes with a mixture of cooked rice, ground meat, and spices, then bake until tender.

**Sandwiches and Wraps**

1. **BLT with Fresh Tomato**: Add a juicy fresh tomato slice to the classic bacon, lettuce, and mayonnaise sandwich.
2. **Tomato and Avocado Grilled Cheese**: A gourmet twist on the classic grilled cheese, with sliced fresh tomatoes and avocado.
3. **Tomato and Feta Wrap**: Fill a wrap with sliced fresh tomatoes, crumbled feta cheese, and mixed greens.

**Breakfast and Brunch**

1. **Tomato and Spinach Omelette**: Whip up an omelette with fresh tomatoes, spinach, and feta cheese.
2. **Tomato and Basil Quiche**: Add fresh tomatoes and basil to a quiche filling with eggs, cream, and cheese.

Enjoy your tomato bounty!
user: That's quite a list! I think I'll try the Bruschetta and Tomato and Mozzarella Salad recipes first. Do you have any tips on how to store fresh tomatoes to keep them fresh for a longer period?
assistant: Bruschetta and Tomato and Mozzarella Salad are both delicious and easy to make.

Now, about storing fresh tomatoes: Here are some tips to help you keep your tomatoes fresh for a longer period:

1. **Store at room temperature**: Tomatoes should not be refrigerated, as the cold temperature can cause a loss of flavor and texture. Instead, store them at room temperature, away from direct sunlight.
2. **Keep them stem-side down**: Store tomatoes with the stem end facing down to prevent moisture from entering the fruit and causing rot.
3. **Don't wash before storing**: Excess moisture can cause tomatoes to spoil faster. Instead, gently rinse them just before using.
4. **Store in a paper bag or breathable container**: Paper bags or containers with holes allow for airflow and can help maintain humidity, keeping tomatoes fresh. Avoid storing tomatoes in plastic bags or airtight containers, as they can trap moisture and cause spoilage.
5. **Use within a few days**: Fresh tomatoes typically last 3-5 days at room temperature. Check on them daily, and use any that are ripe or nearly ripe.
6. **Ripen tomatoes at room temperature**: If you have unripe tomatoes, store them at room temperature, and they will ripen within a few days.
7. **Freeze or can excess tomatoes**: If you have an abundance of tomatoes, consider freezing or canning them to enjoy throughout the year. There are many resources available online for freezing and canning tomatoes.

Some additional tips:

* Handle tomatoes gently to avoid bruising, which can cause them to spoil faster.
* Avoid storing tomatoes near strong-smelling foods, as they can absorb odors easily.
* If you notice any mold or soft spots on a tomato, remove it from the others to prevent spoilage.

By following these tips, you should be able to enjoy your fresh tomatoes for a longer period. Happy cooking!
user: I've been using my fresh tomatoes in salads and sandwiches, but I was thinking of trying to make some homemade pasta sauce. Do you have any tips on how to make a good pasta sauce from scratch using fresh tomatoes?
assistant: Making pasta sauce from scratch using fresh tomatoes is a great way to enjoy the flavors of your garden harvest! Here are some tips to help you create a delicious and authentic homemade pasta sauce:

**Choose the right tomatoes**: For pasta sauce, you'll want to use tomatoes that are high in acidity and have a meaty texture. Plum tomatoes or San Marzano tomatoes are ideal, as they have less water content and more flesh, making them perfect for reducing and creating a rich, thick sauce.

**Roast the tomatoes**: Roasting the tomatoes brings out their natural sweetness and adds depth to the sauce. Cut the tomatoes in half, toss with olive oil, salt, and your choice of aromatics (e.g., garlic, onions, carrots, celery), and roast at 300°F (150°C) for 20-30 minutes.

**Use a flavorful base**: Saute onions, garlic, and sometimes carrots and celery in olive oil until they're soft and fragrant. This mixture, known as "soffritto," is the foundation of many Italian sauces.

**Add the roasted tomatoes**: Once the soffritto is ready, add the roasted tomatoes, salt, and a pinch of sugar to balance the acidity. You can also add a can of crushed or diced tomatoes for added depth.

**Acidity and sweetness**: A good pasta sauce needs a balance of acidity and sweetness. Tomatoes provide natural acidity, while a pinch of sugar helps balance the flavor. You can also add a splash of red wine or lemon juice for added depth.

**Simmer and reduce**: Let the sauce simmer for at least 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, to allow the flavors to meld together and the sauce to thicken. You can help the process by occasionally mashing the tomatoes with a potato masher or the back of a spoon.

**Season and finish**: Taste and adjust the seasoning as needed. You can add herbs like basil, oregano, or parsley for added freshness. Finally, finish the sauce with a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil for a rich, velvety texture.

**Tips and Variations**:

* Use a mixture of tomato varieties for a more complex flavor profile.
* Add meat or vegetables like ground beef, pork, or mushrooms for added heartiness.
* Experiment with spices like red pepper flakes, cinnamon, or nutmeg to create unique flavor profiles.
* Make the sauce ahead and store it in the fridge for up to 3 days or freeze for up to 6 months.

Here's a simple recipe to get you started:

**Fresh Tomato Pasta Sauce Recipe**

Ingredients:

* 2 lbs fresh tomatoes, cored and halved
* 2 tablespoons olive oil
* 1 onion, finely chopped
* 3 cloves garlic, minced
* 1 teaspoon salt
* 1/2 teaspoon sugar
* 1/4 cup red wine (optional)
* Fresh basil leaves, chopped (optional)

Instructions:

1. Roast the tomatoes with olive oil, salt, and aromatics (if using) at 300°F (150°C) for 20-30 minutes.
2. Saute the onion and garlic in olive oil until soft and fragrant.
3. Add the roasted tomatoes, salt, sugar, and red wine (if using) to the pot.
4. Simmer for at least 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has thickened and the flavors have melded together.
5. Taste and adjust the seasoning as needed.
6. Finish with a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil and chopped fresh basil (if using).

Enjoy your homemade pasta sauce!
user: I've heard that using fresh tomatoes can make a huge difference in the flavor of the pasta sauce. I'm excited to try out this recipe. I've been getting a lot of tomatoes from my garden lately, so this is perfect timing. Speaking of which, do you have any tips on how to keep my tomato plants healthy and productive throughout the season?
assistant: Using fresh tomatoes makes a huge difference in the flavor and texture of the pasta sauce. I'm thrilled to hear that you're excited to try out the recipe!

Now, about keeping your tomato plants healthy and productive throughout the season: Here are some tips to help you get the most out of your tomato plants:

**1. Soil and Sunlight**: Tomatoes need well-draining, fertile soil with a pH between 6.0 and 6.8. Ensure your plants receive at least 6 hours of direct sunlight per day.

**2. Water Consistently**: Tomatoes need consistent moisture, especially when fruiting. Water them deeply once or twice a week, depending on weather conditions. Avoid overwatering, which can lead to disease and root rot.

**3. Fertilize Regularly**: Feed your tomato plants with a balanced fertilizer (e.g., 10-10-10 NPK) once a month. You can also side-dress with calcium nitrate to prevent blossom-end rot.

**4. Prune and Train**: Prune your tomato plants to promote air circulation, prevent disease, and encourage fruiting. Remove lower leaves, weak growth, and any suckers (shoots that grow between the main stem and a branch). Train indeterminate varieties using tomato cages, trellises, or stakes.

**5. Mulch and Compost**: Mulch around the base of your plants to retain moisture, suppress weeds, and regulate soil temperature. Add compost to the soil to improve its structure and fertility.

**6. Monitor for Pests and Diseases**: Keep an eye out for common tomato pests like hornworms, aphids, and whiteflies. Regularly inspect your plants for signs of disease like yellowing leaves, black spots, or powdery mildew.

**7. Provide Support**: Tomatoes need support as they grow. Use tomato cages, trellises, or stakes to keep them upright and encourage even fruiting.

**8. Rotate Crops**: Rotate your tomato crop annually to avoid depleting the soil of nutrients and to reduce the risk of disease buildup.

**9. Keep the Area Weed-Free**: Weeds compete with your tomato plants for water, nutrients, and light. Regularly remove weeds to ensure your plants receive the resources they need.

**10. Learn to Identify Common Issues**: Educate yourself on common tomato problems, such as blossom-end rot, cracking, or sun scald, and take action promptly to address them.

By following these tips, you should be able to keep your tomato plants healthy and productive throughout the season. Happy gardening!

Do you have any specific questions about tomato care or common issues you've encountered in your garden?
user: I've been doing my best to keep my tomato plants healthy, but I did notice that some of the leaves started to turn yellow and wilt recently. I did some research and realized I might be overwatering them. So, I cut back on the watering schedule, and it seems to be working - the plants are looking healthier again.
assistant: You're a quick learner! It's great that you noticed the issue, did some research, and took corrective action. Overwatering is a common mistake, especially when we're eager to give our plants plenty of love and attention. But, as you've learned, too much water can be detrimental to their health.

Yellowing leaves and wilting are often signs of overwatering, as the roots start to rot and the plant becomes waterlogged. By reducing the watering frequency, you've given your tomato plants a chance to recover and regain their health.

Now that you've adjusted your watering schedule, keep an eye on your plants' progress and make further adjustments as needed. Remember, it's always better to err on the side of caution and underwater slightly than to overwater and risk root rot.

Some additional tips to prevent overwatering in the future:

1. **Check the soil moisture**: Before watering, stick your finger into the soil up to the first knuckle. If the soil feels dry, it's time to water. If it's already moist, wait another day or two before watering again.
2. **Water deeply but infrequently**: Instead of watering your tomato plants every day, water them deeply once or twice a week. This encourages deep root growth and makes them more resilient to drought.
3. **Monitor weather conditions**: Adjust your watering schedule according to weather conditions. If it's been raining or extremely humid, you may not need to water as frequently.

By being mindful of your watering habits and monitoring your plants' responses, you'll become more attuned to their needs and better equipped to provide the right amount of moisture.

How's your harvest been so far? Are you enjoying the fruits of your labor?
user: I've been enjoying the harvest immensely! I planted 5 tomato plants initially, and they've been producing like crazy. I've been harvesting tomatoes almost daily, and they're so delicious and flavorful. I've been using them in salads, sandwiches, and cooking up a storm in the kitchen. It's amazing how much more flavorful homegrown tomatoes are compared to store-bought ones.
assistant: That's fantastic! There's nothing quite like biting into a juicy, flavorful tomato that you've grown yourself. The taste and texture are unmatched, and it's a great feeling knowing that you've put in the effort to nurture those plants from seed to plate.

I'm thrilled to hear that your tomato plants are producing abundantly and that you're enjoying the fruits of your labor. Homegrown tomatoes truly are a game-changer when it comes to flavor and texture. The store-bought varieties just can't compare to the sweetness and tanginess of a fresh, ripe tomato picked right from your own garden.

It's great that you're using your tomatoes in a variety of dishes, from salads and sandwiches to cooked meals. Tomatoes are so versatile and can elevate any dish with their rich flavor and moisture. Have you tried any new recipes or cooking techniques with your homegrown tomatoes?

Also, I'm curious - what's your favorite way to enjoy your tomatoes? Do you have a go-to recipe or preparation method that brings out their flavor and texture?

responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor
responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.

The user chose to try Bruschetta and Tomato and Mozzarella Salad first.","tags":["cooking","tomatoes","recipes"],"title":"Tomato recipe ideas and user's first picks","valid_from":"2023-05-20T05:52:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["User was excited to try a fresh tomato pasta sauce recipe using garden tomatoes."],"confidence":0.96,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"},{"entity_id":"entity:<6>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<3>","role":"secondary"}],"evidence_kind":"summarized","evidence_quote":"I've been getting a lot of tomatoes from my garden lately, so this is perfect timing.","evidence_reason":"The quoted user text ties the pasta sauce plan to the user's garden tomato harvest, while the assistant recipe details provide the method summarized in the note.","importance":3,"keywords":["fresh tomato pasta sauce","homemade pasta sauce","San Marzano","soffritto","roasted tomatoes"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"The reusable value is the recipe workflow and context, not a single atomic fact; a note preserves the practical cooking method for later recall.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"conversation","text":"Past cooking guidance for making fresh tomato pasta sauce from the user's garden harvest."},"summary":"For homemade pasta sauce from fresh tomatoes, the discussed method was to use meaty acidic tomatoes such as plum or San Marzano, roast them at 300°F (150°C) for 20–30 minutes with olive oil, salt, and aromatics, build a soffritto of onion and garlic, then simmer the roasted tomatoes with salt, sugar, optional red wine, and herbs for at least 30 minutes before finishing with extra virgin olive oil and basil.","tags":["cooking","tomatoes","pasta_sauce"],"title":"Fresh tomato pasta sauce method discussed","valid_from":"2023-05-20T05:52:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["User suspected overwatering caused yellowing and wilting leaves on their tomato plants.","User reduced the watering schedule and the tomato plants looked healthier again."],"confidence":0.99,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<2>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I cut back on the watering schedule, and it seems to be working - the plants are looking healthier again.","evidence_reason":"The quote directly states the corrective action and observed improvement after suspected overwatering.","importance":5,"keywords":["tomato plants","overwatering","yellow leaves","wilting","watering schedule"],"note_type":"lesson","rationale":"This is a troubleshooting lesson with cause, intervention, and result; representing it as a note preserves the reusable pattern better than isolated claims.","scope_json":{"basis":"explicit","kind":"entity","text":"Applies to the user's tomato plants and future troubleshooting of similar yellowing/wilting symptoms."},"summary":"The user noticed yellowing and wilting leaves on their tomato plants, researched the issue, suspected overwatering, cut back the watering schedule, and observed that the plants looked healthier again. This establishes a personal gardening lesson: for the user's tomato plants, yellow/wilted leaves have previously improved after reducing watering.","tags":["gardening","tomatoes","troubleshooting","lesson"],"title":"Tomato plant overwatering lesson","valid_from":"2023-05-20T05:52:00+03:00","valid_to":null}]}
